lens.included_activities: Independent US market-pulp mills that repeatedly supply external paper, paperboard, tissue, specialty-cellulose, or other fiber customers and could transfer as an operating control investment in the target EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: Integrated pulp operations captive to an affiliated paper or paperboard mill, dormant or closure assets, project shells, non-control interests, and mills outside the target band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is tonnage-based commodity or specification-grade pulp sales under spot, formula, and recurring supply arrangements; it is repeat B2B product supply rather than an outsourced service fee.
lens.deviation_from_default: Pulp milling is capital-intensive product manufacturing and often vertically integrated. The lens is narrowed to independent market-pulp suppliers with repeat external customer relationships so the transferable population is coherent.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover NAICS 322100 pulp, paper, and paperboard mills, not stand-alone 322110 pulp mills. I assign AI exposure to maintenance planning, process analytics, procurement, scheduling, compliance reporting, laboratory documentation, sales, and administration while assigning low language-model substitution to continuous-process operators and hands-on maintenance. Stand-alone pulp mills may have an even higher process and maintenance share, which would bias this exposure proxy upward.
a.rationale: In broader mills, 51.68% of employment is production and 13.00% is installation, maintenance, and repair; industrial machinery mechanics alone are 8.06%. These roles operate and preserve hazardous, continuous capital equipment. Higher-wage management, engineering, business, planning, and documentation tasks create a bounded opportunity, but the operational core is not substitutable by generative AI.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from at least three stand-alone market-pulp mills, including control-system automation and maintenance workflows already in use, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Four-digit occupational mix may not represent stand-alone pulp mills.
Existing distributed-control, predictive-maintenance, and process-optimization systems are not separated from not-yet-automated work.

rho.rationale: AI copilots for maintenance knowledge, work orders, process-event analysis, procurement, scheduling, compliance records, and customer documentation are feasible within five years. Integration with legacy control systems, 24/7 uptime, cybersecurity, sparse failure data, process safety, union work rules, and environmental accountability limit implementation. Human operators remain responsible for control decisions and exceptions.
rho.change_evidence: Mill pilots measuring approved recommendations, maintenance hours, unplanned downtime, yield, chemical and energy use, safety events, and compliance deviations over a full maintenance cycle would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No US pulp-mill study directly measures five-year AI implementation.
Apparent optimization gains may come from conventional advanced process control rather than AI substitution.

e.rationale: The eligible population is restricted by the industry's vertical integration, high fixed-asset intensity, commodity product economics, and the recurring-service requirement. Only independent market-pulp mills with external repeat customers, standalone systems, and target-band normalized EBITDA qualify. The injected n of five is already small and estimated, so even one classification change materially affects the share.
e.change_evidence: A complete mill map linking ownership, integration status, customer sales, capacity, normalized EBITDA, environmental liabilities, and control-transfer feasibility would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The injected n is margin-bridged and may be implausible for a capital-intensive mill industry.
A mill may be legally separate but commercially captive to an affiliated paper operation.

s5.bridge: Gallup observes five-year sale or transfer plans across US employer businesses, not pulp mills or completed control transfers. I discount its 22% employer intention rate for a concentrated, capital-intensive, environmentally burdened industry where transfers often occur as corporate portfolio or asset transactions and may not preserve an eligible independent firm. The general survey likely overstates qualifying completion probability here.
s5.rationale: General succession pressure exists because 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older, but pulp-mill transferability depends more on corporate strategy, mill competitiveness, environmental liabilities, fiber access, and customer integration than on small-owner retirement. Qualifying LMM control transfers should therefore be less common than the cross-industry employer intention rate.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed pulp-mill control transactions and closures from RISI/Fastmarkets, PitchBook, Capital IQ, environmental permit ownership changes, and industry advisers would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Cross-industry owner intentions are a weak proxy for corporate mill transactions.
Asset sales, closures, and integrated-company reorganizations may not qualify.

q.rationale: Pulp is sold by tonnage into sophisticated industrial procurement, often against market benchmarks and grade specifications. Operational savings can support the mill through commodity cycles but are likely to be competed into price or absorbed by fiber, chemical, energy, freight, and maintenance volatility. Specialty grades and constrained regional supply can retain more value than commodity market pulp.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, benchmark-price formulas, grade-level contribution margins, cost-pass-through clauses, and cycle-spanning price/cost bridges would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No public evidence directly isolates pass-through of AI-derived savings.
Commodity price cycles can overwhelm modest productivity effects.

d5.bridge: AF&PA reports current wood-pulp consumption and downstream paper-grade production, while BLS projects combined pulp, paper, and paperboard mill output; neither is a five-year constant-quality forecast for independent market pulp. I use the combined industry's roughly flat projected real output as the long-run anchor and recent 3.2% wood-pulp decline as cyclical and structural caution. Integrated production and recovered-fiber shifts can make market-pulp demand weaker or stronger than the aggregate.
d5.rationale: AF&PA reports US wood-pulp consumption fell 3.2% in 2025 as paper and paperboard production declined 3.7%. Printing-writing capacity has fallen sharply, while packaging and tissue remain more resilient. BLS projects combined NAICS 322100 real output slipping from 63.9 to 63.1 billion chained dollars over 2024-34. A modest five-year decline is therefore the center, with upside from packaging, tissue, specialty cellulose, and exports.
d5.change_evidence: Grade-specific five-year market-pulp demand, import/export balances, downstream capacity announcements, recovered-fiber substitution, customer contracts, and mill closure/conversion plans would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Combined pulp/paper output masks stand-alone market-pulp flows.
Demand varies sharply by hardwood/softwood, fluff, dissolving, bleached/unbleached, and geography.

o.rationale: Pulp is a physical intermediate produced through wood preparation, cooking or mechanical separation, washing, screening, optional bleaching, recovery, and environmental controls. Software can optimize these systems but cannot replace the accountable operator that owns, maintains, and complies for the mill. Limited downside reflects customer integration or substitution toward recovered fiber rather than software self-service.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of large-scale customer insourcing, tolling-platform disintermediation, autonomous mills without accountable operating organizations, or rapid recovered-fiber displacement would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This measures need for an operator, not headcount.
Demand loss from grade substitution belongs in d5, not here.

narrative.executive_view: Pulp mills are a poor fit for a recurring outsourced-service roll-up: the industry is capital intensive, frequently integrated, commodity priced, environmentally regulated, and represented by an injected pool of only five estimated LMM firms. AI can improve information and maintenance workflows, but the low compensation-to-receipts ratio and physical process core constrain economic scope.
narrative.ai_impact: Useful applications include maintenance knowledge retrieval, work-order preparation, process-event synthesis, procurement, fiber and chemical planning, laboratory and quality records, environmental reporting, sales documentation, and management analysis. Continuous process operation and hands-on maintenance remain human-accountable and equipment-bound.
narrative.value_capture: Market-linked tonnage pricing and sophisticated industrial buyers expose savings to competition. Specialty grades, uptime, yield, and service reliability may preserve some benefit, but commodity cycles and volatile inputs make attribution and durable retention difficult.
narrative.firm_availability: Only independent market-pulp mills with external recurring supply relationships are eligible; integrated captive operations are excluded. The dataset's five-firm estimate is fragile, and corporate portfolio transactions or mill asset sales often do not create an eligible standalone LMM transfer.
narrative.demand_durability: Packaging, tissue, specialty cellulose, and exports provide durable outlets, while graphic-paper contraction, recovered fiber, capacity rationalization, and recent lower pulp consumption create downside. The five-year center is modest real decline rather than collapse.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central risks are near-zero eligible population, capital intensity, environmental liabilities, commodity pricing, fiber and energy costs, legacy controls, cyber and process safety, mill closures, and composition error from four-digit evidence. One firm can materially change every availability conclusion.
narrative.principal_driver: A 24/7 process plant generates valuable maintenance, planning, quality, and compliance workflows that AI can augment without eliminating the operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: Very few independent target-band firms fit the lens, and commodity pricing plus a low labor share limits retained upside.

sources.title: Pulp, Paper, and Paperboard Mills - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Broader-mill occupation mix: 51.68% production employment and 13.00% installation, maintenance, and repair, including 8.06% industrial machinery mechanics.

sources.title: Universal Industrial Sectors Integrated Solutions Model for Pulp and Paper Manufacturing Industry
sources.supports: Integrated pulp production stages: wood preparation, cooking/pulping, washing, screening, optional bleaching, and paper making, supporting the continuous physical-process characterization.

sources.title: Pulp and Paper NESHAP Plain English Description
sources.supports: Continuous operating-parameter monitoring, recordkeeping, and reporting requirements used to demonstrate ongoing environmental compliance.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry succession proxy: 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% planned a five-year sale or transfer.

sources.title: AF&PA Releases 66th Annual Paper Industry Capacity and Fiber Consumption Survey
sources.supports: 2025 paper and paperboard production fell 3.7%; wood-pulp consumption fell 3.2%; packaging and tissue were more resilient than printing-writing grades.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: BLS projects NAICS 322100 output from 63.9 to 63.1 billion chained 2017 dollars over 2024-34 and employment down 13.1%.
